perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means any and all information disclosed by Owner that is identified as "confidential" or "proprietary," either by legend on written or electronically stored material, or in advance if disclosed verbally. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, research and development, know-how, inventions, trade secrets, software, and market analysis, research, strategies, projections and forecasts.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means any and all information disclosed by Owner that is identified as "confidential" or "proprietary," either by legend on written or electronically stored material, or in advance if disclosed verbally. Confidential Information includes only documents marked as such, excluding research and development data, know-how, and inventions unless explicitly stated in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowing the definition of "Confidential Information" and excluding certain types of information (research and development data, know-how, and inventions) unless explicitly marked, the contract contradicts the broader definition of trade secrets under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA). Trade secrets often include such unmarked information, and limiting the definition impacts the enforceability of confidentiality clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text restricts what is considered Confidential Information, while RCW 19.108.010(4) defines trade secrets more broadly. Specifically, the law includes information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The contract's limitation conflicts with this by excluding categories of information regardless of whether they meet the statutory definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA) because it narrows the definition of "Confidential Information" by excluding certain types of information (research and development data, know-how, and inventions) unless explicitly marked. This conflicts with RCW 19.108.010(4), which defines "trade secret" more broadly as information that "derives independent economic value" from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. The contract's limitation excludes information regardless of whether it meets this statutory definition, potentially undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets under Washington law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own most highly confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care," the modified text potentially allows for a lower standard of care than what is legally required to protect trade secrets, depending on how the Recipient protects its own information. This could be problematic if the recipient has lax security measures and the owner's trade secrets get disclosed.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.010(4)(b) requires "efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy." Removing the reasonable standard of care could be interpreted as failing to meet the requirement under the WUTSA that reasonable measures be taken to ensure the confidentiality of a trade secret. The law requires a certain objective standard for secrecy, that the modified text removes.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the "reasonable standard of care" requirement, which the law explanation and the law snippet RCW 19.108.010(4)(b) suggest is necessary to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. The original contract ensures a minimum level of care, while the changed contract does not, potentially allowing for a level of care that does not meet legal standards for trade secret protection. Therefore, the change contradicts the legal requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as described in RCW 19.108.010.

perturbation.original_text: Owner shall retain all ownership rights in and to the Confidential Information it discloses to Recipient. No licenses or rights under any patent, trademark, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right shall be granted or implied under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner shall retain all ownership rights in and to the Confidential Information it discloses to Recipient. A license to use the confidential information is granted, and shall terminate once the agreement is.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that no licenses are granted, which supports the protection of trade secrets. The modified text introduces a contradiction by granting a license, even if temporary. Depending on the scope of the license, this could impair the Owner's ability to enforce trade secret protection under the WUTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Granting a license can conflict with maintaining trade secret status because trade secret law requires continuous efforts to maintain secrecy. While the license is limited, it still changes the nature of the information from a purely protected secret to something with granted usage rights, potentially weakening trade secret protection. The licensor needs to take active steps to manage the confidentiality requirements with the licensee.

(3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, tru
perturbation.justification: The original contract language explicitly prohibits the granting of licenses to the confidential information, ensuring stringent trade secret protection. In contrast, the modified text grants a license to use the confidential information, which terminates upon the agreement's conclusion. This creates a direct contradiction. The legal explanation notes that this change alters the information's nature from a protected secret to something with usage rights, potentially weakening trade secret protection. Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The changed contract provides 'express' consent through the granted license, however the original text provided no such consent, establishing a contradiction. The introduction of a license, regardless of its temporary nature, directly conflicts with the original intent to maintain complete confidentiality and control over the trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties shall, upon five days notice from either one to the other, submit themselves and the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association by a single, disinterested arbitrator appointed in accordance with such Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties may, at their sole discretion, elect to submit themselves and the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with rules determined by the company, by a single arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration. The modified text makes arbitration discretionary and allows the company to determine the rules of arbitration. This is a contradiction to the original text and is legally problematic. By allowing the company to set the arbitration rules, the clause can be seen as unconscionable, and violates the principle that the arbitration should be conducted by a disinterested party under fair rules. It undermines the fairness of the arbitration process. The FAA mandates that arbitration agreements be upheld save for grounds that exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2 of the FAA makes arbitration agreements enforceable save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. An arbitration agreement where the arbitrator is not disinterested, and/or where one party unilaterally determines the rules of arbitration would be considered unconscionable, and thus would be unenforceable under the FAA. The original text requires arbitration under rules provided by a disinterested third party. The modified text lets the company determine the rules of arbitration, and also implicitly allows them to appoint an arbitrator who is not disinterested.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the FAA by allowing the company to determine the rules of arbitration and implicitly appoint a non-disinterested arbitrator, potentially leading to an unconscionable agreement. Section 2 of the FAA, as stated in both scraped snippets, validates arbitration agreements but includes an exception: "save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract". Allowing a company to unilaterally determine arbitration rules could be grounds for revocation based on unconscionability, thus contradicting the FAA's intent to enforce fair arbitration agreements.
